questions.stem: What does =IF(75>=60, "Pass", "Fail") return?
questions.explanation: IF evaluates the test 75>=60, which is TRUE, so it returns the second argument, the value to use when the test is true, giving Pass.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: How many arguments does the IF function take in its standard form?
questions.explanation: IF takes three arguments: a logical test, the value returned when the test is TRUE, and the value returned when the test is FALSE.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What value does =AND(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) return?
questions.explanation: AND returns TRUE only when every argument is TRUE. Because one argument here is FALSE, the whole expression evaluates to FALSE.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What value does =OR(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE) return?
questions.explanation: OR returns TRUE when at least one argument is TRUE. The third argument is TRUE, so the function returns TRUE.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What does =NOT(5>3) return?
questions.explanation: The test 5>3 is TRUE, and NOT reverses a logical value, so NOT(TRUE) returns FALSE.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: In =VLOOKUP("B", A2:C5, 3, FALSE), the row whose first column equals B has the value 42 in column C. What is returned?
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP finds B in the first column of the range and returns the value from the third column of that same row, which is 42.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: In VLOOKUP, what does the third argument (the column index number) count from?
options: The last column of the range
The first column of the lookup range
questions.explanation: The column index number is counted starting at 1 from the first column of the lookup range itself, not from column A of the sheet, so an index of 3 means the third column of the supplied range.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What does =IFERROR(10/0, "Check input") return?
questions.explanation: The expression 10/0 produces a #DIV/0! error, so IFERROR returns its second argument, the fallback text Check input.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Using =IF(s>=90, "A", IF(s>=80, "B", IF(s>=70, "C", "D"))) with s equal to 82, what grade is returned?
questions.explanation: The first test 82>=90 is FALSE, so the formula moves to 82>=80, which is TRUE, and returns B before reaching the later tests.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: In an exact match VLOOKUP with the fourth argument set to FALSE, what is returned when the lookup value is not present in the first column?
questions.explanation: An exact match VLOOKUP that finds no matching value returns the #N/A error, signalling that the lookup value was not found.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: How does HLOOKUP search for its lookup value?
options: Down the first column of the range
Across the last row of the range
Across the first row of the range
questions.explanation: HLOOKUP, the horizontal lookup, searches across the first row of the range and then returns a value from the row given by the row index number.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: For an approximate match VLOOKUP (fourth argument TRUE), how must the first column of the lookup range be arranged for correct results?
questions.explanation: Approximate match VLOOKUP assumes the first column is sorted in ascending order so it can find the largest value that is less than or equal to the lookup value. An unsorted column gives unreliable results.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: An approximate match VLOOKUP looks up 73 against a sorted first column containing 0, 60, 70 and 80. Which value does it match on?
questions.explanation: Approximate match returns the row of the largest value that is less than or equal to the lookup value. Among 0, 60, 70 and 80, the largest value not exceeding 73 is 70.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Why does =IF(A1<>0, B1/A1, 0) avoid a division error?
options: It only divides when A1 is not zero
questions.explanation: The test A1<>0 is TRUE only when A1 is not zero, so the division B1/A1 runs only in that case. When A1 is zero the formula returns 0 instead, avoiding a #DIV/0! error.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: By default, can a standard VLOOKUP return a value from a column that lies to the left of the lookup column?
options: No, it can only look to the right
Only if the data is sorted
questions.explanation: VLOOKUP searches the first column of the range and returns values from columns to its right. It cannot return a value from a column to the left, since the column index number must be 1 or greater.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A cell holds =IF(AND(hours>40, rate>0), "Overtime", "Normal"). With hours equal to 45 and rate equal to 12, what is returned?
questions.explanation: AND requires both conditions to be TRUE. Here 45>40 is TRUE and 12>0 is TRUE, so AND is TRUE and IF returns Overtime.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What is the purpose of wrapping a lookup as =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(...), "Not found")?
options: It replaces an error result such as #N/A with friendlier text
questions.explanation: IFERROR returns its second argument when the first produces any error. Wrapping a VLOOKUP this way replaces an error such as #N/A with the readable message Not found.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What does =NOT(AND(TRUE, TRUE)) return?
questions.explanation: AND(TRUE, TRUE) is TRUE, and NOT reverses that to FALSE.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A grade formula is written as =IF(s>=70, "C", IF(s>=80, "B", "D")). With s equal to 85, what is returned and why is this a problem?
options: An error, because the tests overlap
C, because the first test 85>=70 is TRUE and stops there
questions.explanation: The first test 85>=70 is TRUE, so the formula returns C and never checks 85>=80. Nested IF thresholds must be ordered from highest to lowest, otherwise a high score is caught by a lower band first.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: What does =OR(score<0, score>100) test for when validating a percentage in the cell named score?
options: Whether the score is a whole number
Whether the score is out of the valid 0 to 100 range
questions.explanation: OR is TRUE if either condition holds. The expression is TRUE when the score is below 0 or above 100, which flags any value outside the valid range from 0 to 100.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: If the fourth argument of VLOOKUP is omitted entirely, which match type does Excel use?
questions.explanation: When the range lookup argument is omitted, VLOOKUP defaults to approximate match, the same as supplying TRUE. This is a common source of wrong answers on unsorted data, so FALSE is usually supplied for an exact match.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: When used directly in a cell, what type of value do the AND and OR functions return?
options: A logical value, TRUE or FALSE
questions.explanation: AND and OR each evaluate their conditions and return a single logical value, either TRUE or FALSE, which is why they are often placed inside the test argument of IF.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A range spans three columns, A2 to C20. What does =VLOOKUP("Item", A2:C20, 4, FALSE) return?
questions.explanation: The column index number 4 exceeds the three columns in the range A2 to C20, so VLOOKUP cannot find that column and returns the #REF! error.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: IFERROR returns its second argument for which kinds of error in the first argument?
options: Any error value such as #N/A, #DIV/0!, #VALUE! or #REF!
questions.explanation: IFERROR catches any error value, including #N/A, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #REF!, #NUM!, #NAME? and #NULL!, and returns its second argument in each case.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible
